3: The nopal (Opuntia ficus-indica (L.) Mill.), known as cactus pear or prickly pear, is cultivated around the world. In the Americas, it is grown in Mexico, Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Chile, Peru and the United States of America; in Africa, nopal can be found in Algeria, Ethiopia, Morocco, South Africa and Tunisia; in Asia, in Jordan, Lebanon, Syria, the Gulf countries, Yemen and Israel; in Europe in

countries like Italy, Portugal and Spain; and in Oceania, mainly in Australia (Ochoa and Barbera, 2017). In most of these countries, nopal is cultivated for commercial fodder and forage (Le Houérou, 1996). Cladodes (edible paddles of flat-stemmed spiny cacti mainly of the Opuntia genus used as vegetables) (Corrales-Garcia et al., 2004) and the fruits (tuna) (Gallegos-Vazquez and Méndez- Gallegos, 2000) are also cultivated for human consumption. The plant is also used for soil conservation (Le Houérou, 1996) and medicinal purposes (Feugang et al., 2006).
* Corresponding author. Departamento de Ingeniería Agroindustrial, Universidad Autónoma Chapingo, km. 38.5 Carretera México-Texcoco, Chapingo, Estado de México 56230, Mexico.

It has been recognized that nopal cladodes (NCs) have potential value for bioenergy production of ethanol, biodiesel or methane (CH4). Kuloyo et al. (2014) suggest that NCs might serve as a second generation feedstock for the production of ethanol. Caballero-

Nolasco (2017), working with NCs and dairy cow manure (DCM) in co-digestion in semi-continuous 10-L laboratory reactors, reported a CH4 yield of 245.6 mL per g of total solids (TS). This amount was obtained using a 75:25 NCs:DCM ratio under mesophilic condi- tions. Nopal plants could also be used in hybrid bioenergy and renewable energy production systems in an integrated solar photovoltaic-nopal system (Cushman et al., 2015).

Zika and Erb (2009) reported that arid and semi-arid areas in the world cover 15.5 and 22.5 M km2 respectively. The use of crassu- lacean acid metabolism (CAM) plants has great potential since they are highly water-use efficient crops. Since nopal is a CAM plant, it is a suitable option for expanding production into semi-arid marginal lands (Owen et al., 2016). Cushman et al. (2015) indicated that the area used for growing CAM plants for both biofuel and food pur- poses could be expanded by developing drought-tolerant bio- energy feedstocks; these plants can be grown in an environmentally responsible manner on abandoned agricultural lands where traditional food crops are typically not cultivated. Depending on prevailing social and economic conditions, nopal can be used as food, bioenergy feedstock, or even both. Mason et al. (2015) reported that up to 5 PW h can be obtained from CAM plants; this would require between 1 and 3.75 M km2 of available semi-arid land, depending on the yield and biogas production as- sumptions used. This amount of energy is similar to the 5.1 PW h of electricity produced annually in the world using natural gas, and more than 50% of that produced by coal (IEA, 2016). CAM plants could help reduce fossil fuel emissions, which in 2010 reached 32 Gt of carbon dioxide (CO2), accounting for 65% of world CO2 emissions (IPCC, 2014).

DCM is the second largest source of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from dairy farms after enteric CH4 ones (Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017). Worldwide, mean GHG emissions related to manure management were 50.9 Mt CO2-eq during 2000-2014 (Dangal et al., 2017). Thus, CH4 production from co-digestion of DCM and NCs could help reduce GHG emissions; Holly et al. (2017) found that anaerobic digestion (AD) reduces CH4 emissions by up to 25%, compared to untreated manure slurries.

Life cycle assessment (LCA) has been recently applied to analyze the environmental impact of energy crops, in particular those used for CH4 production such as sorghum and wheat (Pacetti et al., 2015), and maize silage in mono-digestion (Lijó et al., 2014) or in co-digestion with pig slurry (Bacenetti et al., 2013). The above studies found that crop fertilization is a key factor in emission levels. Field emissions are also influenced by soil type, crop, cli- matic conditions and agricultural management practices. The experimental evaluation of these factors is not usually included in LCA studies due to financial and time constraints (Brentrup et al., 2000).

The sustainability of using different biomass sources to produce alternative energies is the subject of continuing debate. Despite this, different public policies have been enacted in several countries to encourage the production and consumption of different feed- stocks. The NC-DCM combination is a possible alternative for the sustainable production of CH4 through well-proven AD technology. The use of these two feedstocks combines different strengths: a plant that accumulates biomass efficiently, and treatment of DCM which allows reducing GHG emissions. This combination has not been previously studied to determine whether it actually offers an alternative for cleaner energy production. The aim of the present work is to analyze the environmental and energy sustainability of the process, and to identify potential opportunities to reduce the environmental impacts resulting from the use of NCs and DCM as feedstocks. The work is reinforced by the use of experimental re- sults, such as crop emissions measured on site and methane pro- duction quantified in continuous laboratory-scale digesters.

Additional data are from the relevant literature, including: fuel consumption and vehicle emissions (INE, 2005) and CH4 emissions from digestate storage (Baldé et al., 2016). Background data were taken from the Ecoinvent database.

This amount was obtained using a 75:25 NCs:DCM ratio under mesophilic condi- tions. Nopal plants could also be used in hybrid bioenergy and renewable energy production systems in an integrated solar photovoltaic-nopal system (Cushman et al., 2015).

Zika and Erb (2009) reported that arid and semi-arid areas in the world cover 15.5 and 22.5 M km2 respectively. The use of crassu- lacean acid metabolism (CAM) plants has great potential since they are highly water-use efficient crops. Since nopal is a CAM plant, it is a suitable option for expanding production into semi-arid marginal lands (Owen et al., 2016). Cushman et al. (2015) indicated that the area used for growing CAM plants for both biofuel and food pur- poses could be expanded by developing drought-tolerant bio- energy feedstocks; these plants can be grown in an environmentally responsible manner on abandoned agricultural lands where traditional food crops are typically not cultivated. Depending on prevailing social and economic conditions, nopal can be used as food, bioenergy feedstock, or even both. Mason et al. (2015) reported that up to 5 PW h can be obtained from CAM plants; this would require between 1 and 3.75 M km2 of available semi-arid land, depending on the yield and biogas production as- sumptions used. This amount of energy is similar to the 5.1 PW h of electricity produced annually in the world using natural gas, and more than 50% of that produced by coal (IEA, 2016). CAM plants could help reduce fossil fuel emissions, which in 2010 reached 32 Gt of carbon dioxide (CO2), accounting for 65% of world CO2 emissions (IPCC, 2014).

DCM is the second largest source of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from dairy farms after enteric CH4 ones (Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017). Worldwide, mean GHG emissions related to manure management were 50.9 Mt CO2-eq during 2000-2014 (Dangal et al., 2017). Thus, CH4 production from co-digestion of DCM and NCs could help reduce GHG emissions; Holly et al. (2017) found that anaerobic digestion (AD) reduces CH4 emissions by up to 25%, compared to untreated manure slurries.

Life cycle assessment (LCA) has been recently applied to analyze the environmental impact of energy crops, in particular those used for CH4 production such as sorghum and wheat (Pacetti et al., 2015), and maize silage in mono-digestion (Lijó et al., 2014) or in co-digestion with pig slurry (Bacenetti et al., 2013). The above studies found that crop fertilization is a key factor in emission levels. Field emissions are also influenced by soil type, crop, cli- matic conditions and agricultural management practices. The experimental evaluation of these factors is not usually included in LCA studies due to financial and time constraints (Brentrup et al., 2000).

The sustainability of using different biomass sources to produce alternative energies is the subject of continuing debate. Despite this, different public policies have been enacted in several countries to encourage the production and consumption of different feed- stocks. The NC-DCM combination is a possible alternative for the sustainable production of CH4 through well-proven AD technology. The use of these two feedstocks combines different strengths: a plant that accumulates biomass efficiently, and treatment of DCM which allows reducing GHG emissions. This combination has not been previously studied to determine whether it actually offers an alternative for cleaner energy production. The aim of the present work is to analyze the environmental and energy sustainability of the process, and to identify potential opportunities to reduce the environmental impacts resulting from the use of NCs and DCM as feedstocks. The work is reinforced by the use of experimental re- sults, such as crop emissions measured on site and methane pro- duction quantified in continuous laboratory-scale digesters.

Additional data are from the relevant literature, including: fuel consumption and vehicle emissions (INE, 2005) and CH4 emissions from digestate storage (Baldé et al., 2016). Background data were taken from the Ecoinvent database.
8: In order to assess the environmental aspects and potential im- pacts associated with energy produced by co-digestion of NCs and DCM, LCA was used. This study follows the LCA framework stipu- lated by ISO 14,040 standard (ISO, 2006). LCA consists of the following four steps: a) goal and scope definitions, b) life cycle in- ventory, c) life cycle impact assessment, and d) interpretation. OpenLCA 1.6.3 (GreenDelta, 2017), an open source software for LCA and sustainability assessment, was used for LCA evaluation.
9: The case study was based on biogas production from NC and DCM co-digestion, using a covered lagoon anaerobic digester. The characteristics of a digester located in "Rancho la Hondonada", Queretaro, Mexico were used (Fig. 1).

The functional unit (FU) was 1 MJ of bioenergy produced. The inputs to the inventory were detailed according to annual re- quirements. Input production and local transportation were also considered within the system. Material acquisitions and the manufacture and transportation of equipment used in the entire product system were left outside of the system boundary due to their minimal impact per unit of biogas created over the lifetime of the equipment (Hijazi et al., 2016). The inputs, emissions and fuel used during crop establishment were not considered based on the same criteria, because, according to previous tests, crop establish- ment activities contribute less than 1% of the emissions over the crop lifespan. Human labor energy was not considered. No alloca- tion was applied in this analysis, as biogas was considered to be the only product. The digestate is frequently used as crop fertilizer either directly or after treatment, so it is considered to be outside of the boundary system.

11: <header>2.1.1. Description of the systems under assessment </header>
Baseline and scenarios were defined on the basis of the NC production system used and the way in which the digestate was
Fig. 1. Covered lagoon anaerobic digesters at "Rancho la Hondonada", Queretaro, Mexico.

handled. Two different NC production systems were studied: i) conventional farming system (CFS) and ii) organic farming system (OFS); for digestate storage, two types of tanks were used: a) an open digestate storage (ODS) tank and b) a closed digestate storage (CDS) tank. Nopal cladode transport (NCT), dairy cow manure transport (DCMT), pre-processing (PP) and the conversion of the biomass to biogas through AD were considered.

Nopal cladode production: Two different NC production sys- tems were compared: CFS and OFS. Both systems oriented to biomass energy production were planted with the Milpa Alta va- riety at an effective density of 22,988 plants.ha-1 Nopal is a perennial crop with a 20-year lifespan. The first step in crop establishment involved harrowing and plowing the field prior to planting. The nopal was allowed to grow for one year until reaching its productive stage, during which time DCM was applied as fertilization; pest and disease control were conducted manually. These activities were performed for both NC production systems. The main differences between the two systems occur in the pro- ductive stage, particularly in relation to fertilization and weed and pest management.

For the CFS, the fertilization source was an inorganic fertilizer compound that provides nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium, based on urea, triple superphosphate and potassium chloride; weed and pest control were performed using atrazine and insec- ticide respectively. For the OFS, the fertilizer source was DCM produced on site; pest control was performed manually and weed control was carried out both manually and using a mechanical weed cutter.

In both systems, the harvest was done manually. The cropping area for supplying the anaerobic digester was calculated on the basis of the digester's annual NC requirements and annual NC yield volume. It was determined that 2.8 and 1.7 ha are required for the CFS and OFS respectively.

Pre-processing: After harvest, the NCs were transported from the field site to the anaerobic digester. They were ground and filtered and then mixed with DCM at a 75:25 (NC:DCM) ratio (w/ w); the TS were fixed at 3% using water for dilution. The mixer system included an axial mixer.

Anaerobic digestion: The mixture was fed into a 250 m³ anaerobic digester, which was operated in semi-continuous mode, feeding it once a day. The feed mixture flow was adjusted to achieve a hydraulic retention time of 30 d. The anaerobic digester was operated under mesophilic conditions. Fifty percent of the digester liquid was recirculated daily by pumping.

Digestate storage: The digestate generated in the anaerobic digestion process was discharged and stored in a 120 m³ capacity tank. In this step two different digestate storage tanks were stud- ied: ODS and CDS. For the ODS, it was assumed that the CH4 pro- duced during the digestate storage was released into the air, while in the CDS the CH4 produced was recovered and mixed with the biogas from the anaerobic digestion.

16: Data collection and quantification were based on experimental data and complemented with suitable data from the literature and the Ecoinvent database version 3.1 (Wernet al., 2016). The inputs

for NC production were based on data obtained from two sites with different agronomic management, under OFS (Colon, Qro., Mexico) and CFS (Chapingo, Mex., Mexico). Fuel consumption associated with the transportation of agrochemicals, NCT and DCMT were estimated using data for the selected vehicle obtained from the National Ecology Institute of Mexico (INE, 2005). These data are presented in Table 1.

It is assumed that NCT and DCMT were by road with a heavy duty diesel vehicle 8b considering 5.0 and 0.5 km of distance, respectively. Agrochemicals (including inorganic fertilizers, herbi- cides and pesticides) are assumed to be transported 15 km from the local market to the farm using a light duty gasoline truck 1. Addi- tionally, when DCM was a fertilizer source it was transported 5.0 km from farm to field by HDDV8b.

The biogas production inputs and biogas yield volumes were from data obtained in a 10-L anaerobic digester, which was oper- ated with the identical technical parameters mentioned above. The electricity consumption for size reduction was estimated by taking the power consumption, time and capacity of the equipment used into consideration 1.1 h and 2,500 kg respectively). The energy required for mixing, feeding and recirculating was calcu- lated by an energy balance performed in the covered lagoon anaerobic digester located in "Rancho la Hondonada" (Colon, Qro., Mexico). Energy requirements for fertilizer, herbicide and pesticide production are from Ecoinvent database version 3.1 (Wernet et al., 2016).

27: The energy efficiency results of the different scenarios, assessed using EROI, are presented in Fig. 3; it also shows the values of the reference systems reported in the literature for macroalgae (Aitken et al., 2014), straw-cow manure (Pierie et al., 2015) and human feces-corn straw (Wu et al., 2016) used as feedstocks. EROI theo- retically needs to be at least greater than one, although a value of three is recommended as a minimum for a process to be considered energetically sustainable (Hall et al., 2009), since below this level the energy efficiency becomes questionable. The Baseline EROI was

Fig. 3. Energy return on investment (EROI) values for the Baseline, the different sce- narios and the references from the literature (Note 1 - reference values plotted correspond to the best result of each study). Source: 1Aitken et al. (2014), 2Pierie et al. (2015) and 3Wu et al. (2016). (Note 2-the EROI plotted for human feces-corn straw was calculated from scenario 3 of the referenced study, without considering biogas upgrading and waste management and using the low heat value reported by Edwards et al. (2013)).

8.14, while the three scenarios had EROI values ranging from 9.48 to 12.41, all of them above three. The three scenarios also presented better energy performance than the Baseline. The change in NC production from CFS to OFS enhanced the EROI by 49%, whereas the incorporation of CDS (Scenario 2) increased the EROI by only 16.5%. Scenario 3 had an EROI 53% greater than the Baseline, having the positive effect of Scenario 1 and Scenario 2.

In the three reference systems obtained from the literature, two of them (biogas through anaerobic digestion of macroalgae, and biogas from co-digestion of human feces-corn straw) had an EROI below the recommended minimum, whereas all four scenarios in our study were above the minimum level. Scenario 1 and Scenario 3 provided an EROI higher than the value of the biogas produced from straw-cow manure co-digestion, which was 10.9.

The cumulative energy demand (CED) of the different scenarios was calculated based on the FU selected. The specific CED distri- bution of these scenarios is presented in Fig. 4. The contribution of CH4, considered as additional energy recovered from digestate storage, is also displayed. Fig. 4 shows that DCMT has the lowest energy consumption in the Baseline and the three scenarios. The Baseline and CFS have the highest specific energy consumption (52%), compared to the 29% recorded for OFS. This difference is mainly due to the displacement of energy consumption during the production of agrochemicals, mainly inorganic fertilizer, which accounted for 86% of the CFS's CED.

NCT is the second largest contributor to CED in all scenarios, accounting for between 19 and 28% of the total. The CH4 recovered as a result of covering the digested storage brought about a 1.7% increase in the energy produced, which is equivalent to the energy required for the recirculation process.

31: Table 5 shows the total life cycle impact assessment results for the Baseline and the three scenarios, while Fig. 5 includes the contribution of each process to the system. The results show that the main difference is related to NC production, while digestate management does not have an important role in most of the impact categories. The results are discussed below by impact category, focusing on the comparison between the Baseline and Scenario 1. GWP and POCP impact categories were slightly influenced by digestate storage type.
32: As shown in Table 5, all scenarios analyzed can provide a reduction in the GWP category compared to the Baseline. The main influence on the reduction was the use of DCM instead of inorganic

Fig. 4. Contribution of each process to the cumulative energy demand (CED) per functional unit. CFS: conventional farming system, OFS: organic farming system, NCT: nopal cladode transport, DCMT: dairy cow manure transport, PP: pre-processing, AD: anaerobic digestion.
Table 5 Life cycle impact assessment results.

fertilizers. Regardless of the type of fertilizer, covering the digestate storage tank contributed to reducing the GWP. Biogas production with OFS can prevent the emission of 1.72 g CO2-eq which corresponds to a 22.5% GWP reduction, while covering the diges- tate tank resulted in a 2.26% decrease. The combined effect of OFS and CDS resulted in a 24.8% reduction compared to the Baseline.

Fig. 5a shows the distribution of GWP by processes. The AD was the major contributor, generating from 37.8% to 50.3% of total GWP. This contribution was directly associated with the electricity con- sumption, mainly due to the pump used to recirculate the digester liquid. When the PP is considered, the GWP caused by the use of electricity increases from 63.2 to 83.9%. These results agree with those of Collet et al. (2011), who report that the environmental impacts generated by the production of CH4 from microalgae are strongly correlated to the electricity consumption involved. The impact on the GWP category related to the production and use of electricity accounted for 65% of the whole process. Van Stappen et al. (2016) suggest that these impacts could be reduced through electricity generation from biogas.

36: In contrast to GWP, the use of CFS to produce NC was the most favorable in terms of AP. The results (Table 5) show that the sub- stitution of inorganic fertilizer by DCM (Scenario 1) caused an in- crease of 40.7 mg SO2-eq.MJ- or 47.2% compared to the Baseline. This may be mainly due to the association of high NH3 field emis- sions with the use of manure. According to Sommer and Hutchings (2001), field applied manure contributes significantly to the emis- sion of NH3 from agriculture; one way of reducing these emissions is by rapidly incorporating the manure into the soil (Webb et al., 2010).
High power grid usage resulting from those processes requiring electricity also contributes significantly to acidification potential
(Fig. 5b). In relation to biomass transportation, NCT emitted 7.1 mg SO2-eq.MJ-1 in all scenarios, while DCMT emitted 0.25 mg SO2- eq.MJ-1.
37: For EP, the Baseline had the best performance in this category; by contrast, Scenario 1 showed an increase of 45% above the Baseline caused by the fertilizer source substitution (Table 5). The different contribution processes (Fig. 5c) indicated that NC pro- duction was the highest contributor to EP through NH3 field emissions, which represented between 60.3% and 72.7% of the total EP in the whole process.

Since the use of OFS to produce NC in this study had the lowest GWP, but the highest AP and EP, it is necessary to search for alter- natives aimed at reducing NH3 emissions. Sommer and Hutchings (2001) suggest that manure be incorporated into the soil immedi- ately after its application. Webb et al. (2010) recommend injecting the slurry to depths which increase the diffusion path to the soil surface: this leads to most of the denitrified nitrogen (N) being emitted as nitrogen gas (N 2 to avoid increasing N20 emissions.
38: ADP was the impact category with the greatest difference be- tween the Baseline and Scenario 1. According to the results shown in Table 5, the Scenario 1 ADP value was just 2.7% of the corre- sponding Baseline value. CFS was the major process contributor in the Baseline (Fig. 5d). These results have a similar magnitude order to the ones reported by Ribal et al. (2017); these authors investi- gated two citrus farming systems, one organic and one conven- tional. They found that the organic farming system ADP value was only 1.7% of the corresponding conventional production value.

51: Previous LCA studies have shown that there are several pa- rameters that lead to uncertainty when they are performed. Since this study is based on experimental data, this uncertainty can be assumed to be low under the conditions studied (Börjesson et al., 2015). Like other energy crops, biomass yield depends on orchard design (plant density), cultural practices, cultivar fertility and environmental conditions; this last-mentioned factor is an external variable that can lead to an increase or decrease in biomass yield. Nopal is a perennial crop, so its biomass yield depends on plant age and stage of growth. Nopal plants begin to yield 2-3 years after planting; they reach their maximum potential 6-8 years after planting and remain productive for 25-30 years and even longer.

The sensitivity analysis was carried out to determine the influ- ence of biomass yield on the impact categories. The alternatives "high NC yield" and "low NC yield" were based on subtracting and adding 50% of current NC yield data shown in Table 3. The results in Table 6 show that the "high NC yield" alternative had a lower in- fluence on the impact categories than the "low NC yield". The re- sults also show that the overall effect on the environmental impacts was smaller, for most impacts, in scenarios using OFS than CFS, except for AP and EP.

Compared to the original values previously presented in Table 5, the GWP was between 2 and 9% lower considering "high NC yield" and 5-7% higher considering "low NC yield". The AP, estimated for "high NC yield", was 17-22% lower and 51-66% higher with "low

NC yield". The eutrophication potential was 20-24% lower with the "high NC yield" alternative and 60-73% higher with the "low NC yield" alternative. In the other categories, the effect of NC yield was less than 1% in scenarios with OFS, while in scenarios with CFS the effect of NC yield, either high or low yield, was more than 15%.

54: It has been shown that co-digestion of NC and DCM for biogas production was energetically sustainable; the EROI for the Baseline and the three considered scenarios was above the recommended minimum value (3.0).

The NC production under a CFS was the main contribution process in most of the impact categories analyzed. The substitution of CFS by OFS to produce the NC led to environmental impact reduction, except in AP and EP categories. In this context, extending this study in terms of analyzing different fertilizing sources will help elucidate the best practice to avoid negatively affecting the AP and EP impact categories; one option could be the use of digestate instead of DCM or inorganic fertilization. The electricity demand in the systems analyzed also played an important role in the envi- ronmental performance, which was responsible for 28%, in sce- narios with CFS, up to 97%, in scenarios with OFS, of the different impact categories analyzed. In reference to the digestate manage- ment, this had a relatively slight impact, less than 3%, on GWP and POCP, but the methane recovered was comparable to the energy required by the recirculation of the digester liquid in the anaerobic digestion process.

Biogas production from NC-DCM co-digestion and digestate management offers cleaner energy production since the GWP had a lower value than that reported for similar feedstocks and similar. Thus, the use of these two biomasses combines the strengths of a plant that accumulates biomass efficiently and a treatment system that reduces GHG emissions by using one of the main wastes in dairy production.
